meta.name: Trevor Garrett
meta.in_target_population: no — production home builder on a risk-off builder-fee model (~30 homes/yr) in Nashville; neither a documentation-pained sub (Hyp A) nor a risk-taking flipper/BRRRR operator (Hyp B2)

interview_integrity.reliability_note: Eric described only 'a project in the construction space' with no specific solution, so no answer was biased toward a pitched feature. Note Eric introduced himself partway through (after the East Nashville permit story); answers before and after are equally reliable since nothing concrete was floated. The offer to 'stay in contact / help with technology issues' was Eric-initiated relationship-building, not a Trevor commitment.

summary: Trevor Garrett owns and runs Cobalt Ventures, a Nashville builder doing ~30 homes/year plus remodeling and occasional DADU build-outs. His business model is deliberately risk-off: he prefers to build for investors who take out their own loan and carry the deal risk while Cobalt collects a fixed GC/builder's fee (~$100k) every time. He knows Metro zoning well and confirms buildability by emailing the Metro help desk before he starts.

commitment_signals.what: Won a DADU lot-size exception on an East Nashville build by arguing comparable approved neighbors, after back-and-forth with Metro that delayed the start
commitment_signals.verbatim: the other houses in the area have already done it and have been approved and done and finished and so then just a little bit of back and forth and then they're like, yep, we're approving it, you know, because even though you're 74 square feet short on your lot size
commitment_signals.quantified: ~6-month start delay (he downplayed it: 'which isn't much')

commitment_signals.what: Reclassified a West Meade guest house as a 'pool house' to attempt approval; kept getting denied, client held the project, zoning later changed and it passed
commitment_signals.verbatim: I think we had to call it a pool house at first instead and in order to get it passed... then he said, I want to hold off on it because we kept getting denied. And then the zoning changed over there. And then we got it approved
commitment_signals.quantified: client held ~6 months until zoning changed

commitment_signals.what: Routinely emails Metro help desk to confirm what is buildable before starting
commitment_signals.verbatim: I know the zoning... So then I just email the help desk at Metro to confirm it

commitment_signals.what: Has to negotiate wholesalers down on inflated land prices to protect margin on every such deal
commitment_signals.verbatim: then I have to talk them down to a fair price and it's still not fair in my mind
commitment_signals.quantified: alleges wholesalers can make ~$100k on an assignment; inflated example '1.2 vs worth a million'

stated_pains.pain: Wholesalers inflate land-acquisition cost by locking up distressed/off-market sellers below value then assigning the contract to him above value, squeezing his margin and forcing a higher sale price on the end buyer
stated_pains.verbatim: I would say that's the biggest headache... they make, you know, sometimes hundred thousand dollars for doing nothing... it hurts the end buyer too, because then I have the house that I build, I have to sell for more in order to make a profit

stated_pains.pain: Zoning/permit unpredictability on DADUs and accessory structures — lot-size exceptions, use-classification rules, and rules that 'change all the time' — causing intermittent start delays
stated_pains.verbatim: It's a lot of back and forth with the city... the one in West Nashville was delayed eight months until the zoning changed... or maybe not eight months, but at least six months. So it just changes all the time
stated_pains.frequency_stated: three DADU approvals last year; occasional

current_workarounds.what: Email Metro help desk to pre-confirm buildability against known zoning
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Doesn't prevent exception fights or mid-project zoning changes

current_workarounds.what: Cite nearby comparable approved/closed projects to win an exception
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Still requires back-and-forth and delay; outcome discretionary

current_workarounds.what: Reclassify the intended use (guest house -> 'pool house') to fit code
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Can fail (no actual pool); may require waiting for zoning to change

current_workarounds.what: Cultivate one or two trusted wholesalers and negotiate prices down
current_workarounds.why_inadequate: Price 'still not fair in my mind'; structural markup remains

interest_only: Agreed to stay in contact and hear about future projects — Eric-initiated, no commitment
'I'm not against making money... it's just, come on, man' — resigned philosophical complaint about wholesalers/lawyers, not an active search for a fix

jobs_to_be_done.jtbd: Acquire buildable land at a fair price so the build pencils out for the investor and the end buyer
jobs_to_be_done.desired_outcome: Protect builder margin and keep deals viable without paying the wholesaler's middleman markup

jobs_to_be_done.jtbd: Get an accessory/DADU structure approved and start on schedule despite shifting Metro zoning
jobs_to_be_done.desired_outcome: Avoid multi-month start delays and dead deals from exception fights and use-classification rules

inferred_severity.basis: Named unprompted as his biggest headache and ties to real margin loss, but he is largely resigned to it, has a trusted-wholesaler workaround, and frames it as an industry fact rather than something he is actively trying to solve
Real past delays (~6 months twice) but he explicitly minimizes them ('which isn't much', 'isn't a huge deal but it was some time'), does few DADUs, and disqualified himself as the right person to ask

analyst_interpretation.caveats: Severity on p2 is direct from his own framing (he downplayed it). p1 severity is inferred — he stated it is his #1 headache but his resignation and existing workaround argue against high willingness to pay for a tool. Dollar figures (~$100k assignment fees, 1.2-vs-1.0M) are his unverified estimates.

problem_signal.problem_in_their_words: Wholesalers cold-call distressed sellers, lock up property below value, and assign it to him above value — squeezing his margin and forcing the end buyer to pay more; he has to talk them down to a still-unfair price.
problem_signal.relation_to_hypotheses: OUTSIDE current hypotheses — strongest pain (land-acquisition/wholesaler markup) is unrelated to A or B2; permit/zoning pain is real but he downplays it; nothing about scope-change documentation (A) or rehab mis-scoping (B2) surfaced, and his risk-off GC-fee model actively avoids the rehab-budget risk B2 assumes
problem_signal.new_problem_surfaced: Land-acquisition cost inflation via wholesaler assignment markup squeezing builder margin (off-market deal sourcing)

unresolved_questions: How many deals per year actually route through wholesalers vs. direct/investor-sourced, and what is the dollar markup he estimates he overpays annually?
Has he ever spent money on off-market lead sourcing (skip-tracing, direct-mail, PropStream-type tools, a dispo/acquisition hire) to bypass wholesalers?
Across ~30 homes/year, how many starts were actually delayed by permitting last year, and did any delay cost him a deal or fee rather than just time?
best_next_question: Walk me through the last deal where a wholesaler's price made you walk away or nearly killed it — what did you do to find the next lot, and did you spend any money trying to source that deal directly?

And there's an exception for certain like you're only allowed to do it if your lot is a certain size but we had to argue the other houses in the area have already done it and have been approved and done and finished and so then just a little bit of back and forth and then they're like, yep, we're approving it, you know, because even though you're 74 square feet short on your lot size, something like that. Oh, I see. So it was really just like a hard requirement that they maybe you leverage like a personal relationship to get that approved? I don't think we leveraged a personal relationship. And then zoning changed while, like, and then he said, I want to hold off on it because we kept getting denied. And then the zoning changed over there. And then we got it approved. Like you're allowed to do a pool house, but not a guest house or an ABU or whatever you want to call it. So, but they didn't have a pool, so they needed a pool to call it a pool house. So then he just said, let's hold off, you know. And then six months later, zeroing changed and it was allowed in that area. That makes perfect sense. Yeah, well, thank you so much for your time. I think my last question would really just be, like, in terms of your business operations, do you find yourself having to hire out like any consultants or any additional firms that help you finish out a contract or get leads? Like, what's really your largest headache on a day-to-day basis? I would say my biggest headache is wholesalers in general. Are you familiar with wholesalers? Yeah, where they just hold the contract and try to flip it forward. Well, they basically, you know, cold call people and bother people until they can, you know, if someone dies or they kind of search death certificates and divorces and basically anybody that's, you know, and then sometimes sometimes they just get lucky and someone wants to sell a house, but they might call them every month for two years and finally they pay them less than their property's worth and then sell it to me for more than their property's worth. I would say that's the biggest headache and then I have to talk them down to a fair price and it's still not fair in my mind. So maybe wholesalers, I think wholesalers is probably my biggest headache. Gotcha. You got to get like at least one or two you trust, but they're not just completely screwing you over because they don't ever have to put any money down, except they may not put like $5,000 of earnest money down the first 30 days to sell the house. And then once they get under contract, all they do is it's called an assignment. So all they do is essentially look at double close, right? So they don't ever have to, they make, you know, sometimes hundred thousand dollars for doing nothing. Right. Except for years and years of bothering people or getting someone that's about, you know, that's about to die or quick, quick closing, you know, just closing it quick. And I mean, it's a business. Like, I don't, I'm not against making money. It's just, it's like, come on, man. This house isn't worth 1.2. It's worth a million. You know? Yeah. It's like lawyers. You know, there's people out here that actually like generate value in the world. And there's people that just like try to suck out the value everywhere they can. Yeah, but it hurts everywhere. It hurts the end buyer too, because then I have the house that I build, I have to sell for more in order to make a profit. So then the end users always want to get screwed. Oh, I see. And is that part of your personal ventures or is that part of your business model that the house that you build goes under a company name or how does that work? Everyone's different. Some of them, you know, we build about 30 houses a year. Some of them are under our name and we take out the loan. And some of them are an investor that takes out his own loan and pays for it. And then he just essentially pays us to build the house and then he makes the profit. And our business model is to be consistent. So we would rather not have our own personal investments. We would rather, sometimes people give us money to buy the land and do the down payment on the loan and we'll take the loan out. But mainly it's, hey, investor, here's a loan, you know, here's your loan guy, but we've got recommendations. We'll build your house for X as a builder's fee, $100,000, whatever it is. Then they get their own loan, pay the bills throughout the project, pay the GC fee. And then at the end, they sell the house and they might make $300,000, $4,000, or $500,000. Or they might make $100,000, or they might lose $500,000. I mean, that doesn't ever happen, but it can. So they're taking the risk. Our business model is to get our GCC. And we want them to make money so that they'll come back and do two next time or three or four. So they take the risk. They make the money. We get our contractor's fee every single time, no matter what. That's our business model. Yeah, that makes perfect sense.
